item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was actually kind of a sweet story. I liked it. The idea doesn't sound that great but the story and execution were amazing. I guess basic ideas aren't everything. This was great and feels like a file that would be found in some corner of the facility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is quite the anomaly. We have so many skips written up where they're on suicide watch and this one gets special treatment? Hell, she gets to live in a house outside any Foundation site and get special surgery to look like a normal human being. This reminds me of the old SCP-103, before it was changed to be more clinical and aloof. ModernMajorGeneral also makes a point on this being very much like a story involving normal people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 While I think anomalous anorexia is a bit meh in terms of a concept, the presentation and overall story is very well executed. The conflict between the Foundation's imperative to contain and the ethical treatment of SCPs was played out very well. I'd kinda like to know more about the grey-eyed man and the events that made the scip anomalous, but it may distract from the conflict between ethics vs containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for another mention of the grey-eyed man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a commendable portrayal of the Ethics Committee. I like that it's not "we don't need to contain this woman", it's "we don't need to contain this woman in such a negative environment".

I also like that you formally described what 'Class-A amnestics' do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is something of a projection of my own life; as someone who attempted suicide and has been supported in my recovery by my wonderful partner, I really wanted to write an lgbt story with a happy ending. I'm very glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, another lesbian story? This site has so many stories with gay characters…and I am completely okay with that! Can never have too many gays as I always say. I don't know why so many authors here make their characters some flavor of lgbt, but it is one of the many reasons I keep coming back. This one made me tear up at least. And a happy ending to boot. Wonderful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She clearly had a mental illness before her run-in with the anomalous. Does the fact that she can't eat now mean she can't be diagnosed with a mental illness that she clearly had and still has? Just because she can't eat doesn't mean her mental illness doesn't still cause her a lot of grief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note to a mod who might see this: this message is completely empty. Chrome wigged out on me a little and this ended up being submitted with a single word as you can see by looking through this comment's history. Please delete this if you don't mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a really fun read. Always nice to see some of the more "mundane" SCPs that aren't eldritch abominations trying to eat people, but just regular beings trying to go about their lives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this didn't really do much for me, im going to have to no-vote, sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I shed a tear there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the above commentary, but beneath those valid points, I think this is a pretty good casting of a solution to the question: when would containment be superseded by the Foundation's ethical principals? I'd love to see people's take on that and I think this would be one of the better possible premises.

Edit: Also, I think this is immaculately organized. The article is so tidy and I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I just wanted to write a story about a mental health struggle with an uplifting ending, because I've been struggling with my mental illness a lot lately. Maybe this wasn't the right place for it, or the right way to go about it.

Maybe in a few weeks, when I finish exams and such, I'll have the time to look back over this and rework it a bit. Until then, I completely understand your perspective and respect your vote, and thank you for your honesty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed.

From a technical standpoint, this also makes no sense. Someone with paranormal abilities of not requiring nutrition and being unable to eat food shouldn't receive a diagnosis of anorexia nervosa, it becomes meaningless. It's like saying that someone who spontaneously combusts if they leave their house has agoraphobia. This person could have food phobia, or some kind of unspecified eating disorder, but applying an eating disorder label to someone who cannot eat is just silly.

Having said that, this is not really that different from a real person with anorexia, which is not a good thing in this case. The Foundation probably wouldn't bother with a case like this - while theoretically impossible, there are enough True Weird Medical Stories (You Won't Believe #3!) involving someone who never ate any food but survived for many years that the Foundation would ignore them.

More importantly, the author has presumably wanted to write something about mental illness and eating disorder, but it's not using the genre well. The Broken Masquerade stuff does this by drawing an allegory between mental illness and the difference/othering of having other various anomalies/superpowers/whatever (see Confiscated Documents March 2022 etc). This, however, is just… someone with mental illness with some Foundation setting window dressing. If there are no meaningful sci-fi/paranormal components, you would be better off reading/writing about actual real people with mental illness, particularly given the superficial nature of this character. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't get into yet another humanoid pity party.

I'm so very tired of SCPs which give their central character one primary characteristic of suffering terribly, and don't really do anything else to develop them in the slightest. I can't be arsed to care for the plight of something engineered solely to elicit pathos in such an indelicate manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very sweet. The kind of sweet that makes you feel good when you read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, that part was alluding to SCP-2039. As excerpted:

…It's all I thought about for a couple years there. It was about that time I got a visit from the gray-eyed man.

Wilkes: Gray-eyed man?

P01: Yessir. Eyes as gray as a thundercloud. Said he was some kind of collector, or storyteller or somethin'…

So credit to dierubikdie for that. I needed some source for Olivia's anomaly, but I wanted something vague, because this story is about Olivia and her struggle, and the devil, or whoever it is, is only there in the past to set it up. The gray-eyed man caught my eye and I decided to use that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, when I saw the image, I was afraid it would rely heavily on shock factor, but it ended up being surprisingly wholesome!

item.title: big +1
item.content:encoded: I really like social examinations of containment, and this is one of the best ones

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, an article that recognize that the Foundation is cold and logical, but not cruel, and this feels incredibly real.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A man with grey eyes. I don't know, he was… he's kinda, I can't… it's like, he's, fuzzy. I… he said he could help. He called it a bargain. When I woke up… I could only cry out in pain, at first.

…The devil?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like it could be an actual, honest to god document in an SCP site

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice look at containment from the anomaly's point of view, and some nice EC intervention. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a nice skip.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I've been working on this one for a while, and I finally worked up the nerve to post it to the mainsite after getting lots of positive feedback.

I know it's not going to work for everyone, and if you don't like it, I understand. This was really something of an emotional project for me; Olivia's story mirrors my own attempted suicide and mental health struggle over the last year. I have been helped so much by my partner and their loving support in my recovery, and I really wanted to write something about getting better and getting that happy ending.

Thank you all so much for making this site a wonderful community and great environment for me to pursue my writing. I wish you all the best.

-ashbury

Image Attribution
Close

"File photo" was edited by me. Original image is in the public domain, as are the signatures: 1, 2, 3, 4
